title: Resolving the phase space
abstract: Quantum tomography can reconstruct fine phase-space structures that are not necessarily resolved by measurement itself. We show that the effective resolution of tomography is determined by a sampling operator linked to the Gram matrix of the measurement, which defines the experimentally accessible degrees of freedom and reconstruction bandwidth of the quantum state. Acting analogously to a transfer function in imaging, this operator provides an operational criterion for distinguishing genuinely resolved quantum features from artefacts induced by incomplete sampling or reconstruction assumptions. Reconstruction in the Gram eigenbasis emerges as an efficient measurement-adapted compression of the tomographic problem. Within finite frame theory, the same structure appears naturally as the frame operator. Our results establish a resolution-based framework for quantum tomography relevant for contemporary experiments probing highly structured nonclassical states.
summary: This paper introduces a resolution-based framework for quantum tomography, arguing that the true information content of a quantum state is limited by the measurement apparatus. By analyzing the Gram operator, the authors provide a diagnostic tool to distinguish genuinely resolved quantum features from artifacts introduced by incomplete sampling or model assumptions. This guides experimentalists toward efficient, data-adapted reconstruction methods.
main_problem: The core problem is determining which features of a reconstructed quantum state are genuinely supported by limited experimental data versus those that are merely artifacts of the reconstruction assumptions.
main_result: The effective resolution of quantum tomography is governed by a sampling operator derived from the measurement's Gram matrix, providing an operational criterion to distinguish true quantum features from noise or model artifacts.
method: The authors develop a resolution-based framework by analyzing the Gram operator, which dictates the experimentally accessible degrees of freedom, and propose reconstruction in the Gram eigenbasis for efficiency.
model_or_system: The work applies to quantum state characterization using Quantum Tomography, often simulated using homodyne tomography of structured nonclassical states like cat states.
key_observables: The Gram operator ($G$) and its eigenvalues ($\lambda_k$), which quantify the effective reconstruction bandwidth and the reliability of individual Hilbert-space modes.
important_parameters: The numerical rank of the Gram operator, which determines the effective number of reliably accessible degrees of freedom.
main_assumptions: The primary limitation is the finite measurement resolution, and the framework cautions that apparent resolution might stem from model assumptions rather than actual data sensitivity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the eigenvalue spectrum of the Gram operator defining the bandwidth, and compare reconstruction fidelity using the data-driven Gram eigenbasis against fixed bases (like the Fock basis), showing the failure of low-dimensional truncations in the latter.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the limitations of standard tomography to introducing the Gram operator as the key diagnostic tool. It then develops the concept of reconstruction in the Gram eigenbasis as a measurement-adapted compression, culminating in a resolution-based framework.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for AMO and quantum optics as it provides a rigorous, data-driven diagnostic tool for interpreting experimental measurements of complex quantum states, moving beyond simple reconstruction algorithms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Eigenvalue spectrum of the Gram operator in state space G (left panel) and the corresponding operator-space Gram matrix Q (right panel) for homodyne tomography of a cat state. The spectrum of G defines the effective reconstruc- tion bandwidth and quantifies how uniformly Hilbert-space modes are sampled by the measurement. The operator-space spectrum Q governs conditioning and noise sensitivity of the inversion. Reduced detection efficiency induces rapid spectral decay, demonstrating that only a limited subset of modes is experimentally resolvable.

FIG. 3. Repeated MaxLik reconstructions of the Wigner func- tion performed in subspaces spanned by the 2 (upper pan- els), 3 (middle panels), and 11 (lower panels) dominant eigen- modes of the Gram operator. Different panels within each row demonstrate the effect of statistical noise on weakly sampled modes. Already a few dominant Gram modes yield faithful and relatively stable reconstructions, showing that the exper- imentally accessible structure of the cat state is captured in a remarkably low-dimensional subspace. Enlarging the re- construction bandwidth permits finer phase-space details to appear, but increasingly amplifies noise sensitivity and over- fitting of unsupported components.

FIG. 2. Left panel: Wigner function of the target even cat state. Middle panel: reconstruction fidelity versus the di- mension of the subspace defined by the dominant eigenmodes of the Gram operator G. Right panel: reconstruction fi- delity versus reconstruction dimension in the Fock basis. The Gram basis reaches high fidelity within a remarkably low- dimensional subspace: only three dominant modes are suffi- cient, whereas approximately ten Fock states are required for comparable reconstruction quality.

Figure 1 shows the eigenvalue spectra of the Gram op- erator G and the operator-space Gram matrix Q for sim- ulated homodyne tomography. The spectrum of G de- termines the effective reconstruction bandwidth by iden- tifying the experimentally accessible modes, whereas Q quantifies the quadratic conditioning structure governing noise sensitivity in operator space. Figure 2 compares the exact Wigner function of the target cat state with the reconstruction fidelity

FIG. 4. For comparison, repeated MaxLik reconstructions of the Wigner function are performed in Fock subspaces spanned by the 2 (upper panels), 3 (middle panels), and 11 (lower panels) lowest Fock states. Different panels within each row correspond to repeated reconstructions from independently generated noisy datasets. Low-dimensional Fock truncations fail to reproduce the characteristic structure of the target cat state, while larger reconstruction spaces become increasingly sensitive to statistical fluctuations.
